close_reason: Positive observation: put() ordering guarantees no data loss
description:
put() at lib.rs:381-447 deliberately collects existing object handles BEFORE creating the new one (line 399-405), creates the new object (line 414-419), zeroizes the plaintext copy in the template (line 424-428), then destroys the old objects (line 436-438). This ordering guarantees:

  - create_object failure → original secret intact.
  - create_object success + destroy failure → new value is live, returns Ok, duplicate cleaned up next put.
  - Data loss is impossible.

A future contributor reading this might "simplify" by destroying the old object first ("clearer flow") — that introduces a window where create can fail and the secret is gone. Defend the order.

Also good: the post-create zeroization of the template Vec at line 424-428 is subtle (the secret bytes have been copied into the HSM by C_CreateObject, but the Vec<u8> in the template is still live in process memory until the closure ends). Explicitly zeroizing here is correct; without it, the secret would sit on the heap until the closure drops.

A future contributor might "simplify" by removing the explicit zeroize, reasoning "the Vec drops at end of closure anyway" — this is wrong because Drop of Vec<u8> does NOT zeroize. Defend this.
title: put() ordering (find existing -> create new -> destroy old) guarantees no data loss; defend it
